body_txt: In a situation where a security camera recording of an event is reviewed, especially if the audience already saw it happen, by the purest coincidence it will be the same footage that the audience has already witnessed . This creates a slight bit of confusion on how an in-universe device (especially a stationary device, like a fixed security camera) could have captured and produced such, well, cinematic footage. Even if not recycled footage, there are other elements used to produce similar results; that is to say it will include all the same dramatic camera angles, pans and zooms as can be expected of a professional film crew, including: The general excuse is an effort to save money on making realistic, unique footage of the scene. Sometimes this is Hand Waved by overlaying visual effects on the recording — conversion to black-and-white , simulated scanning lines, a date/time stamp or watermark, meaningless computerese , advanced technology where cameras can be anywhere ( with real time access ), the all-powerful Enhance Button and so on; this still doesn't change the fact that the movie isn't attempting to recreate the scene as it would actually appear to the in-universe device. This can be averted with actual science fiction technology, at the minimum 20 Minutes in the Future , where a camera is actually recording holographic information in three-dimensional space , allowing the characters to choose their own angle to observe. It's still usually Stock Footage , though. Generally a subtrope of Artistic License – Film Production , as you know the film crew understands where a camera can realistically be placed. See also Magical Computer , Caught on Tape . Compare Surveillance as the Plot Demands , where the footage is equally implausible but due to location (How did they get a camera on the moon?) and not necessarily the angles. For security cams that are actually magical, see Magical Camera .
